conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 10 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can help you determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me ask you a few questions:

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me. He lives with his mum.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand the situation a bit better.

Do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep? For example, do you pay maintenance or support costs for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out. I contribute towards his upkeep - things like helping with clothes and bits and pieces for school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Just to clarify - does Alex's mum (or anyone else) currently claim Child Benefit for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, his mum claims Child Benefit for him already.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me explain the outcome:

**You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. Child Benefit is generally paid to the person the child lives with, as they are considered to have the main responsibility for the child's day-to-day care.

Since Alex lives with his mum and she is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you cannot also claim it - even though you contribute towards his upkeep. If you wanted to claim Child Benefit for Alex, his mum would need to stop her claim first, and you would need to demonstrate that you have the main responsibility for Alex (which typically means he would need to live with you).

Your contributions towards Alex's costs are valuable and important, but they don't create eligibility for Child Benefit when another person (his primary carer) is already claiming.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 10 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex does not live with the claimant - he lives with his mother. Although the claimant contributes towards Alex's upkeep (clothes and school items), Alex's mother already claims Child Benefit for him. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any time, and it is generally paid to the person the child lives with who has main responsibility for their care. Since the mother is already claiming and Alex lives with her, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 10), who lives with his mother. While the claimant contributes to Alex's upkeep, the mother already claims Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and priority goes to the person the child lives with. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible.
